questions.question_text: How long must a Montana broker retain transaction documents?
questions.explanation: Per ARM 24.210.601, Montana brokers must retain trust account records and real estate transaction documents (sales contracts, leases, options, agency agreements, closing statements) for 8 years from the later of the date of receipt or the date the transaction was completed. The 8-year period matches the statute of limitations on written contracts under MCA 27-2-202(1). Records may be paper or electronic and must be readily available for Board inspection.

questions.question_text: A salesperson leaves Broker Quinn's office. Who is responsible for retaining that salesperson's transaction records?
questions.explanation: In Montana, the broker is responsible for retaining all transaction documents, even those created by salespersons who have since left the office. This responsibility lasts the full 8-year retention period required by ARM 24.210.601.

questions.question_text: Can a Montana broker store transaction records electronically?
distractors: No, only paper originals are accepted
questions.explanation: Montana permits electronic storage of transaction documents. Whether paper or electronic, all records must be readily available for Board inspection for the required 8-year retention period under ARM 24.210.601.

questions.question_text: A listing agreement is terminated by the seller. What must the broker do with the transaction documents?
distractors: Hold them until a new listing is signed
Send them to the Board for archival
questions.explanation: When a listing is terminated, the Montana broker must provide copies of all relevant documents to the seller. The broker must also retain their own copies for the 8-year retention period under ARM 24.210.601.

questions.question_text: The Board of Realty Regulation requests transaction files from a broker. How quickly must the broker make them available?
questions.correct_answer: Available for inspection at all times
questions.explanation: Montana requires that transaction records be readily available for Board inspection at any time. There is no advance notice requirement. Brokers must maintain organized records that can be produced promptly.

questions.question_text: Who may hold a trust account for real estate transactions in Montana?
questions.explanation: Montana trust accounts must be in the broker's name only. Salespersons, office managers, and other staff cannot hold trust accounts. The broker bears sole responsibility for trust funds.

questions.question_text: A Montana broker opens a trust account at an out-of-state online bank. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, must be at a Montana financial institution
distractors: Yes, if the bank has a Montana branch
Only with written Board of Realty approval
questions.explanation: Montana requires trust accounts to be held at a Montana financial institution. An out-of-state online bank, even if FDIC-insured, does not satisfy this requirement.

questions.question_text: A Montana broker keeps $150 of personal funds in the trust account to cover bank service fees. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, the maximum is $100 for bank fees
distractors: Yes, any amount for bank fees is fine
No, zero personal funds are permitted
questions.explanation: Montana allows a broker to maintain up to $100 of personal funds in a trust account solely to cover bank service fees. The $150 exceeds this limit and constitutes improper commingling.

questions.question_text: What designation must appear on a Montana broker's trust account?
questions.explanation: Montana requires trust accounts to be clearly designated as trust or escrow accounts at the financial institution. This distinguishes them from operating or personal accounts and protects client funds.

questions.question_text: Broker Harris deposits her personal commission check into the trust account alongside a client's earnest money. What violation is this?
questions.explanation: Depositing personal funds into a trust account is commingling, which is prohibited in Montana. The only exception is up to $100 maintained to cover bank fees. Commission checks must go into the operating account.

questions.question_text: Under Montana rules, how soon must a broker deposit earnest money into the trust account after receipt?
questions.explanation: Per ARM 24.210.426, Montana requires earnest money deposits to be placed in the trust account within 3 business days of receipt, unless the contract specifies an alternative timeline.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract states earnest money will be deposited within 7 days instead of the standard timeline. Is this acceptable?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the contract specifies otherwise
distractors: No, 3 business days is always required
questions.explanation: While Montana's default rule is 3 business days, the purchase contract may specify an alternative deposit timeline. If the contract addresses timing, its terms govern the deposit deadline.

questions.question_text: Trust funds earned as commission become due to the broker. How quickly must they be withdrawn?
questions.explanation: When trust funds become due to the broker as earned commission, Montana requires withdrawal within 10 business days. Leaving earned funds in the trust account beyond this period is improper.

questions.question_text: How often must a Montana broker reconcile trust account records?
questions.explanation: Montana requires monthly reconciliation of trust account records. The broker must compare bank statements against their records to ensure all funds are properly accounted for.

questions.question_text: A transaction falls apart and both buyer and seller claim the $10,000 earnest money deposit. What must the broker do?
distractors: Release funds to the seller automatically
Split the funds equally between parties
Return all funds to the buyer immediately
questions.explanation: When earnest money is disputed, a Montana broker cannot unilaterally disburse the funds to either party. The broker must hold the funds until the parties reach agreement or a court orders disbursement.
